content.text:
*"The secret things belong to the Lord our God, but the 
things revealed belong to us."*{Deuteronomy 29:29}{Cryptography is the art of creating mathematical assurances for who can do what with data.}{Ronald Rivest}

Number theory, once considered the purest of pure mathematics, now has 
vital practical applications in cryptography, computer science, and 
coding theory.
Many identification numbers include a **check digit** to detect errors. Modular arithmetic makes this possible.

content.title: ISBN-10 Check Digit
content.problem:
An ISBN-10 $d_1 d_2 \ldots d_{10}$ satisfies:
\[ \sum_{i=1}^{10} i \cdot d_i \equiv 0 \pmod{11} \]

For ISBN 0-201-31452-?, find the check digit.
$$
&1(0) + 2(2) + 3(0) + 4(1) + 5(3) + 6(1) + 7(4) + 8(5) + 9(2) + 10(?) \\
&= 0 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 15 + 6 + 28 + 40 + 18 + 10x = 115 + 10x
$$
We need $115 + 10x \equiv 0 \pmod{11}$, i.e., $10x \equiv -115 \equiv 6 \pmod{11}$.

Since $10 \equiv -1 \pmod{11}$: $-x \equiv 6$, so $x \equiv 5 \pmod{11}$.

Check digit: 5.

content.text:
The **RSA cryptosystem** is the foundation of secure 
internet communication. Its security rests on the difficulty of factoring 
large numbers.

content.title: RSA Key Generation
content.content:
1. Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$.

2. Compute $n = pq$ and $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.

3. Choose $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.

4. Compute $d$ such that $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

5. **Public key**: $(n, e)$. **Private key**: $d$.

content.title: Simplified RSA
content.problem:
Let $p = 5$, $q = 11$. Then $n = 55$ and $\phi(55) = 4 \cdot 10 = 40$.

Choose $e = 3$ (coprime to 40). Find $d$: $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Since $3 \cdot 27 = 81 = 2 \cdot 40 + 1$, we have $d = 27$.

To encrypt $M = 7$: $C = 7^3 \mod 55 = 343 \mod 55 = 13$.

To decrypt: $13^{27} \mod 55 = 7$. $\checkmark$

content.text:
**Hash functions** map data to fixed-size 
values, often using modular arithmetic.

content.title: Simple hash
content.problem:
Hash function: $h(k) = k \mod 13$.

Keys 27, 13, 55, 40 hash to: $h(27) = 1$, $h(13) = 0$, $h(55) = 3$, $h(40) = 1$.

Note: 27 and 40 **collide**—they hash to the same value.

marginNotes.text:
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter; 
to search out a matter is the glory of kings.
